metadata.summary: Quality attribute conflicts (ISO 25010), architecture style trade-offs, CAP theorem, and communication pattern trade-offs for informed architecture decisions.

content.title: Quality Attribute Trade-offs in Architecture
content.summary: Quality attributes often conflict: improving one typically degrades another. This methodology covers the ISO 25010 model, common attribute conflict pairs, architecture style trade-offs (monolith vs microservices), CAP theorem data trade-offs, and communication pattern trade-offs as the factual basis for any trade-off decision.

why.p: Every architecture choice involves sacrificing one quality to gain another. Without a shared model of which attributes conflict and why, teams make decisions based on gut feel or fashion (cargo-culting). The ISO 25010 quality model provides a systematic vocabulary for quality requirements. CAP theorem and architecture style trade-off tables provide empirically grounded reference points that prevent reinventing these trade-offs from scratch on each project.

list.item: When building quality attribute scenarios for ATAM or decision matrices — to identify which pairs are in tension.
When evaluating architecture style options (monolith, microservices, serverless, event-driven).
When selecting a data storage strategy and consistency requirements must be weighed against availability.
When designing communication patterns between services (sync vs async vs event-driven).
When writing the "Consequences" section of an ADR — to enumerate explicit gain/loss pairs.
As a substitute for context-specific analysis — these tables are starting points, not verdicts. Your system's actual trade-offs may differ.
When a single quality attribute clearly dominates all others for your use case — simplify instead of balancing a full ISO 25010 analysis.

section.name: iso-25010
section.p: ISO 25010 Product Quality Model defines eight top-level quality characteristics, each with sub-characteristics:

list.item: Functional Suitability — completeness, correctness, appropriateness.
Performance Efficiency — time behavior, resource utilization, capacity.
Compatibility — co-existence, interoperability.
Usability — learnability, operability, user error protection, accessibility.
Reliability — availability, fault tolerance, recoverability, maturity.
Security — confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, authenticity, accountability.
Maintainability — modularity, reusability, analyzability, modifiability, testability.
Portability — adaptability, installability, replaceability.

section.name: common-conflicts
section.p: Improving one quality attribute typically degrades another. Common conflict pairs:

list.item: Performance vs Maintainability — optimized code is harder to change; abstractions add call overhead.
Performance vs Portability — hardware-specific optimizations reduce portability.
Security vs Usability — authentication steps and access controls create friction.
Security vs Performance — encryption, validation, and audit logging add latency.
Availability vs Consistency — in distributed systems, maintaining both under partition is impossible (CAP).
Availability vs Cost — redundancy and failover infrastructure multiplies costs.
Flexibility vs Performance — dynamic dispatch, plugin architectures, and late binding add overhead.
Flexibility vs Simplicity — configurability increases cognitive load and bug surface.
Scalability vs Consistency — horizontal scaling requires relaxing strong consistency guarantees.
Scalability vs Cost — infrastructure to handle 10x load costs substantially more.

rule.statement: Every architectural decision that improves a quality attribute MUST explicitly name which other attribute(s) it degrades and whether that trade-off is acceptable for the current system context. The "Consequences" section of every ADR MUST include a gain/loss pair for each affected attribute.
rule.rationale: Hidden trade-offs (not documenting sacrifices) is the most common cause of production failures that "nobody predicted". Making them explicit converts implicit organizational knowledge into reviewable artifacts.

section.name: architecture-style-tradeoffs
section.p: Architecture style trade-offs — what each style gains and loses:

list.item: Monolith — gets: simplicity, debugging ease, fast local development, single deployment, easy transactions. Loses: independent scaling of components, team autonomy at large scale.
Modular Monolith — gets: boundary clarity, simpler operations than microservices, testable modules in isolation. Loses: deployment independence (still one artifact), full team autonomy.

Microservices — gets: independent scaling, team autonomy, technology heterogeneity, isolated failures. Loses: operational complexity, distributed system latency, debugging difficulty, data consistency.
Serverless — gets: no ops burden, automatic scaling, pay-per-use cost model. Loses: cold start latency, vendor lock-in, difficult local development, limited execution duration.
Event-driven — gets: loose coupling, extensibility, resilience to downstream failures. Loses: eventual consistency, complex debugging, harder to trace causality.

example.title: Architecture style decision for fintech startup
example.code:
Context: Series A fintech, 8 engineers, MVP in 6 months, PCI-DSS required, 10K tx/day.
ATAM-style scenario analysis: Scenario 1: PCI-DSS Audit (H, H) Monolith: single scope, larger blast radius → Medium risk Microservices: isolated PCI scope to payment service → Low risk
Scenario 2: Developer Onboarding (M, L) Monolith: single codebase, easy to understand → Low risk Microservices: multiple repos, complex local setup → High risk
Scenario 3: 50x Traffic Spike (H, H) Monolith: vertical scaling only → High risk Microservices: independent scaling of hot services → Low risk
Decision: Modular Monolith + extracted Payment Service (hybrid) Trade-offs accepted: Get: fast development velocity, minimal PCI scope, clear module boundaries, easier onboarding Lose: some future extraction effort, two deployment pipelines, not full service independence

section.name: cap-theorem
section.p: CAP Theorem: a distributed system can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance simultaneously. Since network partitions always occur in distributed systems, the practical choice is between CP and AP.

list.item: CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance) — gets: correctness, no stale reads. Loses: availability during network partitions. Examples: PostgreSQL clusters, CockroachDB, etcd.
AP (Availability + Partition tolerance) — gets: always available even during partitions. Loses: immediate consistency (eventual consistency model). Examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB (default), Couchbase.
CA (Consistency + Availability) — possible only on a single node with no partitions. Not a realistic distributed option.

rule.statement: Every data storage decision MUST state which CAP trade-off is acceptable for the domain. Payment systems and financial ledgers MUST use CP databases. User preference, activity feeds, and session data CAN use AP databases. Mixed requirements MUST use a hybrid: CP for transactional data, AP for read-optimized projections.
rule.rationale: Choosing AP for a payment ledger causes data loss on partition recovery. Choosing CP for a social feed adds unnecessary latency. Mismatched CAP choice is the most common source of data consistency incidents.

list.item: Synchronous (REST, gRPC) — gets: simplicity, immediate response, easy debugging, strong consistency. Loses: coupling (caller blocked on callee), cascade failures, scaling bottlenecks at slow callees.

Asynchronous (message queues) — gets: decoupling, resilience (caller continues when callee is slow/down), independent scaling. Loses: complexity, eventual consistency, harder to debug, need idempotency.

Event-driven — gets: very loose coupling, extensibility (add consumers without modifying producers), high throughput. Loses: eventual consistency, complex causality tracing, operational overhead of broker.

example.title: Hybrid sync/async order processing
example.code:
Context: E-commerce platform, 10K orders/day, payment gateway timeouts causing failures.
Quality attribute impact of options: | Attribute | Sync | Full Async | Hybrid | Saga | |----------------|-------|------------|--------|-----------| | Consistency | Strong| Eventual | Mixed | Eventual+compensation | | User latency | High | Low | Medium | Low | | Complexity | Low | High | Medium | Very High | | Reliability | Coupled| Decoupled | Mixed | Decoupled |
Chosen: Hybrid - Sync: validate + reserve inventory (2s timeout) - Async (Kafka): payment, shipping prep, notification
Trade-offs accepted: Get: fast checkout response (<500ms), resilience to downstream failures, independent scaling Lose: immediate payment confirmation, strong consistency, simple debugging
Implementation requirements from trade-off: - All async handlers MUST be idempotent - Dead letter queue for failed processing retry - WebSocket for real-time order status

section.name: llm-prompt
section.prompt:
Analyze quality attribute trade-offs for [architectural decision].
Current system: [Brief description of current state] Proposed change: [Description of proposed change]
For each quality attribute pair that may conflict: 1. Identify the trade-off (what improves, what degrades) 2. Quantify impact if possible (percentages, response times, etc.) 3. Assess if trade-off is acceptable for [context] 4. Suggest mitigations for negative impacts
Focus especially on: - Performance vs Maintainability - Security vs Usability - Consistency vs Availability - Flexibility vs Simplicity
Conclude with: Is this trade-off profile acceptable for a [type of system]?
